planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained, humus-rich, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade; Alabama azalea is a shallow-rooted woodland plant that requires well-drained, acidic soil and cannot tolerate alkaline, compacted, or waterlogged conditions.

directions: Alabama azalea is a rare native deciduous azalea endemic to the limestone cedar glades and sandy, acidic woodlands of central Alabama and adjacent states — one of the most sweetly fragrant of all native azaleas, its white to creamy-white flowers with yellow-orange blotches emit a delicate, spicy-sweet fragrance reminiscent of lemony honey that fills the woodland in spring. It blooms in April after the piedmont azalea but before leaves emerge, producing large, showy clusters of flowers at a time when the garden's background is still relatively bare. Alabama azalea is adapted to the warm, humid climate of the upper Gulf Coastal Plain, tolerating zone 9 summers better than most native azaleas of eastern North America — an important advantage for Gulf Coast native gardens where heat and humidity defeat many Rhododendron species. In acidic woodland gardens and under tall pines, plant in combination with native groundcovers like Carex, Pachysandra procumbens, and native ferns for an authentic Alabama woodland garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained to moderately wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; hammock sweet azalea tolerates wet, shaded conditions better than most azaleas — it naturally grows in moist hammock forests and swamp margins.

directions: Hammock sweet azalea is a large, summer-blooming native azalea of the Gulf Coastal Plain — the most heat-tolerant and latest-blooming of all eastern native azaleas, producing fragrant clusters of tubular, white (occasionally pink-tinged) flowers with a clammy, sticky texture in July and August, when summer heat makes gardening difficult and most flowering shrubs have long since finished blooming. Its natural habitat is the moist, shaded understory of hardwood hammocks, bay swamps, and magnolia-dominated wet forests from Florida to Louisiana, making it the most shade- and moisture-tolerant of all Gulf Coast native azaleas. In Gulf Coast landscape gardens, it fills a critical niche as a large, fragrant, summer-flowering shade shrub for moist, acidic woodland conditions — pair with ferns, Louisiana irises, and swamp spider lily in moist, acid-soil shrub borders for a true Gulf Coast hammock garden. The late summer bloom time and fragrant flowers attract hummingbirds and giant native sphinx moths that are still active in summer heat.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in moist to dry, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy, well-drained soil in partial shade; coast azalea is one of the most drought-tolerant of all native azaleas once established and thrives in the sandy, acidic coastal plain soils of the Atlantic Seaboard.

directions: Coast azalea is a compact, stoloniferous native azalea of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain's sandy pine barrens and oak scrublands — producing one of the most intensely fragrant flowers of any native azalea, with white to pale pink blooms that emit a strong, sweet vanilla-clove fragrance in April and May before the leaves fully expand. Unlike most native azaleas that form upright, single-stemmed shrubs, coast azalea spreads slowly by underground stolons (horizontal stems) to form broad, low colonies — a growth habit that makes it one of the finest native azaleas for use as a naturalized groundcover or mass planting under open pinelands and dry oak canopy. Its compact, low stature (rarely over 4 feet) and tolerance of dry, sandy, nutrient-poor acidic soils make it more versatile in the landscape than taller species; it is also more heat-tolerant than northern azaleas and performs reliably in the upper Gulf Coastal Plain. Several hybrid azaleas have been developed from this species for their fragrance and compact habit.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in average, well-drained to moist, adaptable soil in full sun to partial shade; cockspur hawthorn is extremely adaptable to a wide range of soils and moisture conditions and is one of the most drought-tolerant and pollution-tolerant of all native hawthorns.

directions: Cockspur hawthorn is a native small tree or large shrub of the eastern United States named for its long, curved thorns (3–4 inches) that resemble a rooster's spur and made it one of the most effective barrier hedges historically used to create impenetrable property boundaries before barbed wire. Its extremely wide, horizontal branching creates a distinctive, picturesque silhouette that provides year-round interest: white flower clusters in May, glossy red fruits persisting through winter, and spectacular scarlet-to-orange fall foliage color. As a wildlife tree, it is exceptional: the long-persistent red fruits are consumed by cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, robins, and bluebirds through winter when other food is scarce; the dense, thorny structure provides the safest possible nesting habitat for catbirds, thrashers, and wood thrushes. A thornless cultivar ('Inermis') is available for locations where the formidable thorns would be a hazard, providing all the wildlife and ornamental benefits of the species without the thorns.
sources: USDA Forest Service Silvics of North America

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall in average, adaptable soil in full sun; Washington hawthorn tolerates a wide range of soils including alkaline, clay, and occasionally wet conditions — one of the most urban-tough of all native hawthorns.

directions: Washington hawthorn is the most widely planted of all native North American hawthorns in cultivation — an exceptionally ornamental small tree with multiple seasons of interest: dense clusters of white flowers in May, glossy, deeply lobed leaves turning orange-red in fall, and abundant clusters of small bright red fruits that persist into winter. It is one of the highest-value wildlife trees available for the eastern garden: at least 20 species of birds consume the fruits, including cedar waxwings, robins, and bluebirds; the dense, thorny structure (1–3 inch curved thorns) provides nesting habitat for catbirds, thrashers, and kingbirds; and over 150 species of Lepidoptera use native Crataegus as larval host plants. Washington hawthorn adapts to urban conditions including soil compaction, air pollution, and alkaline pH better than most native landscape trees, making it suitable for street planting, parking lots, and urban parks where native trees are rarely successful. A top choice for combining year-round ornamental interest with maximum wildlife value in limited space.
sources: USDA Forest Service Silvics of North America

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions or transplants in fall in average to dry, well-drained to moist, alkaline or acidic soil in partial shade; snowberry is one of the most shade-tolerant of all native fruiting shrubs and thrives under forest canopy where other fruiting shrubs fail.

directions: Snowberry is a low-growing, thicket-forming native shrub of North American forests and woodland edges, producing the remarkable clusters of pure white, waxy berries (technically drupes) in fall that gave the plant its name — the berries are striking against the bare winter stems, remaining on the plant through November and December as one of the last fruits visible in the winter woodland. The berries are technically mildly toxic to humans (containing saponins) but are consumed by birds including ruffed grouse, pheasants, and robins, as well as by black bears and other mammals; they were used medicinally by several indigenous tribes. Snowberry spreads freely by stolons to form dense, erosion-controlling thickets — excellent for stabilizing slopes and difficult, shaded problem areas where other shrubs struggle to establish. The small, pink tubular flowers in summer are a critical nectar source for hummingbirds and are also visited by native bees; the twinberry hummingbird (Calypte anna) in the west is particularly associated with Symphoricarpos species as a nectar plant.
sources: USDA Forest Service Silvics of North America

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root transplants in fall in average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to partial sun; meadow rose is nearly thornless compared to most native roses and adapts to a wide range of moist to moderately dry soil conditions.

directions: Meadow rose is the most cold-hardy and one of the most nearly thornless of all North American native roses — its species name 'blanda' means smooth, mild, or agreeable, referring to the nearly unarmed (spineless) character of mature stems that make it among the more garden-friendly of all native roses for areas near walkways. Fragrant, five-petaled, medium-pink flowers appear in June on arching stems, followed by small, orange-red hips that persist through winter and are consumed by ruffed grouse, mockingbirds, and a remarkable variety of songbirds. Native to the eastern and north-central United States from Maine to Colorado, it forms slowly spreading colonies by suckers and is among the first plants to colonize disturbed sites and woodland edges, providing excellent wildlife cover and erosion control. Like all native roses, it is host to native mining bees (Halictus and Andrena species) that collect rose pollen as a primary food source, and many moth species use Rosa as a larval host plant. Excellent for naturalizing in prairie and meadow borders, hedgerows, and native garden edges.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions or transplants in fall in dry to average, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; bush honeysuckle is extremely adaptable to dry, rocky, or poor soils and is one of the most shade and drought tolerant of all native flowering shrubs.

directions: Bush honeysuckle (Diervilla) is an easy-to-grow native flowering shrub of eastern North American rocky woods and dry slopes, producing small but cheerful, tubular yellow flowers in summer that turn orange-red as they age, providing an attractive bicolored display on the same plant; the compact habit and beautiful foliage that turns brilliant orange-red in fall add to its landscape value through three seasons. Despite sharing the 'honeysuckle' common name with the invasive Japanese honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica), Diervilla is a completely different and well-behaved native genus that poses no invasive risk and is in fact declining in parts of its range due to habitat loss. It spreads slowly by underground stolons to form colonies — useful for erosion control on shaded, dry slopes where few other shrubs perform reliably — and tolerates dry shade better than almost any other flowering native shrub, including spots under shallow-rooted maples and in the dry, poor shade near building foundations. Hummingbirds and native bumblebees visit the flowers, and the autumn foliage provides late-season color in woodland garden situations.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Carolina rhododendron is more drought tolerant than most rhododendrons but still requires consistently moist, acidic soil — mulch heavily to maintain moisture and protect shallow roots.

directions: Carolina rhododendron is one of the most compact and garden-worthy of all native North American rhododendrons — a small-leaved, dense, evergreen shrub from the high-elevation heath balds and rocky slopes of the southern Appalachians that produces masses of rose-pink to white flower clusters in April and May that can cover the entire plant in bloom. It is notably more heat-tolerant, drought-adaptable, and pest-resistant than the large-leaved rhododendrons (R. maximum and R. catawbiense) and adapts to typical garden conditions more readily; its compact size (2–6 feet) makes it appropriate for small-space native gardens and foundation plantings where large rhododendrons would overwhelm the space. The tiny, dark-spotted dots (puncta) on the leaf undersides give it the alternate name 'punctatum' and help identify it in the field; the small, leathery evergreen leaves roll upward in cold temperatures as a water-conservation mechanism, creating a distinctive winter appearance. One of the best native rhododendrons for shaded rockeries, acidic slopes, and native woodland gardens in the mid-Atlantic and upper South.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant young container-grown shrubs in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; rosebay rhododendron tolerates deeper shade than almost any other flowering broadleaf evergreen — it naturally grows in the dark understory beneath hemlock and hardwood canopy.

directions: Rosebay rhododendron (great laurel) is the largest native rhododendron in North America — capable of reaching 20–35 feet in the moist, cool, deep shade of Appalachian stream gorges and hemlock forests — and produces massive trusses of white to pale pink, spotted flowers in June and July, a month later than most other rhododendrons, making it the last and largest-flowered of all native flowering shrubs in the eastern forest. Its enormous, glossy, dark green leaves (up to 10 inches long) curl tightly during cold weather as a frost protection mechanism, a distinctive winter character; the dense, multi-stemmed growth creates impenetrable evergreen thickets in shaded stream valleys that are critical winter shelter for ruffed grouse, white-tailed deer, and black bears. It is the most shade-tolerant of all native rhododendrons — thriving in the darkest understory beneath eastern hemlock, where little else with ornamental value grows — and represents one of the few truly shade-tolerant, large-scale evergreen flowering shrubs for the eastern native garden. Grows slowly but long-lived; once established it requires minimal care in appropriate conditions.
sources: USDA Forest Service Silvics of North America
